CSCE 181 Syllabus

Section 599 (41801)

Introduction to Computing

Spring 2026 - College Station

Course Information

Meeting Times:  Meeting Type: LEC

Meeting Days: TR

Start Time: 12:45PM

End Time: 2:00PM

Start Date: 01/12/2026

End Date: 05/05/2026

Meeting Location:  ONLINE ONLINE

Credit Hours:  1

Instructor Details

Aakash Tyagi

Email:  tyagi@tamu.edu

Office Location:  PETR-207

Phone:  979-845-5480

Office Hours

By appointment via e-mail

Preferred Contact Method

e-mail: tyagi@tamu.edu

Webpage:  https://engineering.tamu.edu/cse/profiles/tyagi-aakash.html

Biography

Aakash Tyagi has a PhD in Computer Engineering in the field of

Semiconductor Yield Modeling and Design for Manufacturability. He

has over two decades of industry experience at Intel Corporation in

end-to-end CPU development including front-end logic design,

functional verification, physical design, circuit design, and silicon

debug and validation. Since 2014, he has been with Texas A&M

University Department of Computer Science and Engineering as a

Professor of Practice where he teaches courses in Computer

Organization, Systems Programming, and Software Testing and

leads research and workforce development activities in hardware

verification.

Catalog Description

Introduction to Computing. (1-0). Credit 1. Introduction to the broad field of

computing; presentations from industry and academia about how computer

science concepts are used in research and end products; includes a major writing

component.

Additional Course Details

Course Objectives:  The objectives of this course are to inform

students about the field of Computer Science and Engineering, and

to introduce them to the wide range of exciting applications of

computation and technology in society. Important terms and

concepts in the field will be introduced, as a preview to what will be

learned in other courses. We will explain the motivation for various

aspects of our majors, including the core course sequence in our

curriculum, math requirements, supporting area, co-ops, etc.

Invited speakers will give guest lectures on current topics which will

be used to give students an overview of different areas within the

broad field of Computing.

Course Learning Outcomes

learned in other courses. We will explain the motivation for various

aspects of our majors, including the core course sequence in our

curriculum, math requirements, supporting area, co-ops, etc.

Invited speakers will give guest lectures on current topics which will

be used to give students an overview of different areas within the

broad field of Computing.

Course Learning Outcomes

Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:

explain how computation influences many aspects of our

technological society.

explain how computer science is about algorithms, not just

programming.

be familiar with important terms and concepts in the field.

understand the rationale for the sequence of courses required

of our majors.

understand different aspects of being a software engineer.

Special Course Designation

None

Textbook and/or Resource Materials

This material Is: Optional

Understanding the Digital World

Authors:  Brian W. Kernighan

Publisher:  Princeton University Press

Publication Date:  2017

Grading Policy

Course grade is based on attendance. Details are as follows:

Total 12 sessions

You must attend the seminar  individually  by  logging into

ZOOM  ( https://tamu.zoom.us ) with your  TAMU NETID .

Visit  https://tamu.zoom.us/profile  to make sure both

your  first and last names  match TAMU records.

For each session, we log seminar attendance on ZOOM

(1=Present, 0=Absent)

The course grading scale is as follows:

A  →  # of 1’s = 11  𝑜𝑟  12

B  →  # of 1’s = 9  𝑜𝑟  10

C  →  # of 1’s = 7  𝑜𝑟  8

D  →  # of 1’s < 7

Absence Exceptions: Up to 2 ‘absent’ or ‘0’ may be converted

into “present” or “1” by submitting a written 1-page report of

the session recording(s) to Canvas. Example: Say, a student

attended 10 sessions. Then they may write a 1-page report of

any one of the two missed sessions to qualify for an A.

Late Work Policy

Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not

considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy ( Student Rule 7 ).

Course Specific Late Work Policy

By default, the course grading is solely based on attendance.

However, as stated above in the grading policy, up to TWO

absences may be 'made-up' by submitting written one page reports

of sessions. Recordings of each session will be posted on Canvas on

a weekly basis under "Assignment" category that allows file

uploads.  All submissions must be made no later than

11:59pm Tuesday April 28.

Now, a few words on the written 1-pagers:  main expectation

is that you  are able to capture key bits that are presented in the

absences may be 'made-up' by submitting written one page reports

of sessions. Recordings of each session will be posted on Canvas on

a weekly basis under "Assignment" category that allows file

uploads.  All submissions must be made no later than

11:59pm Tuesday April 28.

Now, a few words on the written 1-pagers:  main expectation

is that you  are able to capture key bits that are presented in the

talk and your key learnings. I believe these things can be

efficiently explained in a 1-pager. The details of font size and

spacing are not important.

Course Schedule

Total 12 sessions on a variety of topics. For any given week, the

announcement about the topic and speaker will be made by

Monday of the week of each session. The sessions are planned

weekly Tuesday as follows:

Jan 13, Jan 20, Feb 3, Feb 10, Feb 17, Feb 24, Mar 17, Mar

24, Mar 31, Apr 7, Apr 14, Apr 21.

Course Specific Attendance Policy

Attendance Policy:  Grade depends on attendance (stated

above). Please do not send a note to the instructor or grader if you

plan to miss a class or just forgot to attend. The grade policy

stated above places both the burden and empowerment on you.

Communication:  Students are responsible for checking the

announcements regularly for class updates on Canvas.

Academic Honesty:  The Aggie Honor Code is: “An Aggie does not

lie, cheat, or steal or tolerate those who do.” Upon accepting

admission to Texas A&M University, a student immediately

assumes a commitment to uphold the Honor Code, to accept

responsibility for learning, and to follow the philosophy and rules

of the Honor System. For additional information please visit:

www.tamu.edu/aggiehonor/

For this course, the honor code will be upheld as follows:

Students are expected to adhere to the honor code in being

present on Zoom for the entirety of the presentation portion

of the session.

Course Specific Makeup Work Policy

By default, the course grading is solely based on attendance.

However, as stated above in the grading policy, up to TWO

absences may be 'made-up' by submitting written one page reports

of sessions. Recordings of each session will be posted on Canvas on

a weekly basis under "Assignment" category that allows file

uploads.  All submissions must be made no later than

11:59pm Tuesday April 28.

Now, a few words on the written 1-pagers:  main expectation is that

you are able to capture key bits that are presented in the talk and your key

learnings. I believe these things can be efficiently explained in a 1-pager.

The details of font size and spacing are not important.
